publications.editedComment: Punch up the boundary: this is an avatar resolver, not an editor. The optional `userId` wins over the active account id, and the optional username wins over the store username when building the image alt text. With no usable id, the Component shows `/images/kindart.webp`; otherwise it awaits `userStore.userImage(id)`. A watcher reruns that lookup when the effective id or relevant ArtImage id changes. If the loaded URL fails in the browser, the image element is switched to the same fallback. No upload, crop, cache mutation, or profile save occurs here. One round image, one reactive lookup, one dependable escape hatch.

This greenhouse has labeled beds for the whole server record: title, label, type, access mode, base URL, endpoint and health paths, category, model, authentication, visibility flags, description, and private notes. Changing server type fills only missing defaults for COMFY, A1111, OpenAI, Ollama, or Anthropic. Submission first asks the Server store to save the form; an API key is updated separately only after an existing server id is available and the field is nonempty. Stored keys can also be cleared explicitly. The form reports partial key-update failure instead of pretending the entire save failed. Good cultivation: defaults assist, but do not uproot values already planted.

BAM—one forge, two save operations. The main form edits `serverStore.serverForm` and refuses submission without title and base URL. Type presets hammer in missing endpoint, health, access, auth, and category values without overwriting existing metal. `saveServer` persists the record first; only an existing id plus a nonblank local key triggers `updateServerApiKey`. That split allows the UI to report ‘server saved, key update failed’ honestly. A separate control clears the stored key and resets auth to NONE. Show/hide affects only the local input. The Component shapes configuration; the Server store owns persistence and closing the form.

The field guide receives its complete ordered slot list from the parent. Each slot is keyed by rarity number; caught entries contain a Butterfly record, while uncaught entries remain null and show their padded number. The header counts caught specimens by filtering those non-null records. Caught slots draw a small four-wing SVG from the Butterfly colors, expose the name as the title, gain active styling, and emit `inspect` with that exact record when clicked. Empty slots emit nothing. This Component does not fetch the roster or merge user catches itself—the source comments assign that patchwork to the parent. A tidy display seam with no hidden collection state.

The data contract is one ordered `slots` array whose entries pair a rarity number with either a Butterfly record or null. `caughtCount` is derived locally from non-null entries. The template maps every slot to a stable rarity key, renders caught colors into an inline SVG, and emits one `inspect` event only when a caught record is clicked. Uncaught entries display their ordinal and remain inert. The parent is explicitly responsible for joining the full Butterfly roster with the user's catches before passing data down. This keeps the link graph clean: parent owns acquisition and merging; gallery owns counting, presentation, and the inspection signal.

The net here is exactly one strand. `butterfly-layer` renders `startup-animation` and declares no props, emits, state, store access, event handlers, or additional layout. Every question about timing, visibility, interaction, cleanup, or butterfly motion belongs to that child Component, not this wrapper. Its value is positional: callers can mount the startup experience through a stable butterfly-layer name while the implementation remains delegated. Nothing is caught, persisted, or coordinated in this file. The boundary is small enough to inspect completely, which is preferable to inventing responsibilities for an empty layer.

Tiny wrapper, sharp boundary. The entire template is `<startup-animation />`. There is no script block, no local style, no Butterfly store, no prop, no emitted event, and no fallback branch. Therefore this Component cannot honestly claim to animate, select, catch, or dismiss anything by itself; it merely names the place where the startup-animation child is mounted. That may be useful for registry stability, but the behavior must be reviewed in the child source. A single leaf is still a leaf. Do not call it a whole hedge.

Two gatherings share this portal. A discovery Butterfly from the store opens a congratulations card whose backdrop or button calls `clearDiscovery`; its colors, name, message, and discovery copy come from that record. Separately, the optional `butterfly` prop opens a capture card with rarity, message, speed, wing frequency, color swatches, and confirm or cancel choices emitted to the parent. Both cards Teleport to the body, transition under the same modal name, and render their wings from inline SVG shapes. The Component does not add a capture to the gallery itself. Store-owned discovery and parent-owned capture remain distinct even when both use the same sky.

Two modal pixels, two ownership paths! Discovery mode reads `butterflyStore.discoveryButterfly`, paints its wing colors, and closes through `clearDiscovery`. Capture mode reads the `butterfly` prop, displays rarity, speed, wing frequency, message, and color swatches, then emits `confirm` or `cancel` instead of mutating a collection. Backdrop clicks dismiss through the matching owner, while clicks inside each card stop propagation. Both variants Teleport to `body` and use the same transition shell, but they are not interchangeable state. The source draws every specimen with inline SVG; no image request or persistence call is hidden in the modal. Crisp separation, bright wings.
